narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much heart and determination it takes to pursue this path, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.40, an LSAT score of 25, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 2, a middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time. The model assessed your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try at 48%, which unfortunately crossed the threshold that led to your application's rejection—law schools use this prediction to prioritize candidates predicted to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factors pulling toward a failure prediction were your Year 3 decile ranking and LSAT score, as both are notably lower than what's typical for students who pass (where averages hover around the 5th or 6th decile and much higher LSATs). These stood out strongly because they deviated from the stronger academic standings seen in most successful bar takers. On a more positive note, your middle family income actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing compared to lower-income peers who face steeper odds. Your lower Year 1 decile had a milder helpful effect, as did your undergraduate GPA and full-time enrollment plans, which align with the majority of students (over 90%) and slightly supported a passing outcome. Overall, your academic metrics, especially later in school and on the LSAT, carried the most weight in tipping the scale toward this concerning prediction when stacked against typical admitted students who boast higher rankings and scores. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how your numbers compared to patterns in past bar exam data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or programs, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience as a Black woman overcoming challenges. Many students in similar situations have improved their profiles and succeeded—reach out to admissions advisors for targeted prep resources, and reapply stronger. You've got this; your journey is just getting started.
